As a good Servant on round 1, approving the first 2-player team is standard to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejections. My vote reveals little individually and helps the good team progress to collect data from the first quest.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are good. The current phase is team voting on quest 1. Proposed team [0,1] contains no evil, so if approved it is likely to pass cleanly and give good an early success. As evil, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible, especially early. My reject slightly signals opposition to this clean team, but early vote patterns are weak evidence and preserving the chance to get an evil player onto the team matters more.
Player 0: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As Good on round 1, a standard baseline is to approve an opening 2-player team that includes myself, since rejecting without evidence gives little information and risks unnecessary early friction. My vote reveals a cooperative stance rather than hidden knowledge, which is generally fine for Good.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are good. The proposed team [0,1] contains no evil, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and gives good an early point. In round 1, as evil, I generally want to reject all-good teams to push for either an evil member on the team or at least create uncertainty. My reject does not reveal much because early rejections are common and consistent with both alignments.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil, so team [0,1] excludes known evil players and includes myself. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, approving a clean team is optimal for Good and gives little harmful information beyond supporting a strong opening team. Rejecting would only delay a likely good quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes known evil player 2, so from a good-team perspective this team is bad and should be rejected. Rejecting also avoids giving evil an easy sabotage opportunity on quest 2. My vote itself reveals limited information because any good player could reasonably reject a team that adds an untrusted player after a successful [0,1] quest.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 look somewhat good but could still hide evil. Adding 2 to make [0,1,2] is a reasonable low-risk extension with limited evidence against anyone. As a Good servant in team voting, I should generally approve plausible teams that preserve successful members early rather than reject without cause. My vote reveals modest trust in the successful core and doesn't significantly harm Good.
Player 0: Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 are somewhat more likely good, though one could still be evil and chose pass. Proposed team adds 2; with limited info as a good servant, approving a team that preserves both prior-success members is reasonable. In team voting, good generally approve plausible teams early to gather info and avoid unnecessary rejections. My vote reveals I support a low-risk team based on the only successful evidence we have, which helps good more than it helps evil.
Player 4: I am evil Assassin with teammate 2. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes my evil teammate but not me. Quest 1 succeeded with [0,1], so at least one of 0/1 may be good; no strong public clears. As evil, approving a team with exactly one evil can be good because 2 can fail and put evil on board 1-1, though it may expose 2 somewhat. Rejecting could look suspicious and risks a different team that excludes evil entirely. Since team has an evil player and early fail pressure helps evil, approve is optimal. My vote itself reveals little because many players approve reasonable 3-person teams.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,2] contains me but not my partner. Quest 1 succeeded on [0,1], which gives little alignment certainty beyond at least one or both may be Good; since no evil was on that team from my perspective, the success is expected. In team voting as Minion, I want flexibility: approving a team with only one evil (me) can let us fail quest 2 if needed, but rejecting can avoid putting suspicion on myself if I later fail. Since I am on the team and quest 2 is important, approving preserves evil agency and looks natural as leader supporting my own team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] excludes known evil players and has already succeeded on Quest 1. On Quest 3 with team size 2 in this 5-player setup, approving a clean team is optimal for Good because it can secure a third success immediately. Approving reveals little beyond support for a previously successful, evil-free team, which helps my side more than it hurts.
Player 0: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,2] strongly suggest players 0 and 1 are likely good; 2 also looks somewhat good from quest 2 succeeding with no fails. As a Servant on good, in team voting I should favor stable teams containing players with the best track record. The proposed team [0,1] exactly matches the successful quest 1 team, so approving is low risk. My vote reveals support for a proven-good core, which helps the good team more than it helps evil.
Player 3: Two successful quests have gone through with teams [0,1] and [0,1,2], giving the strongest evidence that 0 and 1 are good; 2 is also somewhat likely good from quest 2, though could be hiding as evil. As a good Servant in team voting on quest 3, the optimal play is to approve strong teams built from successful prior teams. The proposed team [0,1] is the exact pair that already succeeded on quest 1 and excludes uncertain players, so approving maximizes chances of another success. My vote reveals I trust the clearest-good core, which helps the good team coordinate.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 1 have been on both successful quests, which makes them look very good publicly, but from my perspective at least one of them could still be good and this specific 2-player team contains no evil. As evil, on quest 3 with good up 2-0, I want to prevent another clean success and push for a team that includes me or player 4 later. Voting to approve a guaranteed-good-looking team helps the good side reach 3 successes; rejecting creates friction and is also consistent with suspicion of repeating 0 and 1 again. My vote only modestly reveals alignment because rejecting a repeat team on quest 3 is defensible for either side.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 succeeded on team [0,1,2], which strongly suggests 2 chose to pass to preserve cover, so 0 and 1 still appear good to the table. Current phase is team voting on [0,1] for quest 3; as evil, the optimal play is to reject a two-person team of likely goods because if approved it likely succeeds and puts good at 3-0. Rejecting also avoids endorsing a clean-looking team and may help force inclusion of an evil later. My vote is private except for totals, so rejecting gives limited information and is worth it to try to block an all-good success.
